docs.title: NMCB hand-over documentation
docs.text:
Welcome to the working hand-over for the Netherlands ME/CFS Cohort and Biobank (NMCB) data management and data infrastructure role.

This documentation is written to make the role:

  • transferable
  • reproducible
  • easier to maintain
  • less dependent on tacit knowledge

docs.title: How this site is organised
docs.text: Section Purpose Data Lifecycle How data flows: collection → storage → cleaning → modelling → distribution Systems Reference documentation for Castor, devices, scripts, Snowflake, biobank Operations & Governance Recurring tasks, data requests, SOPs, escalation

docs.title: How to use this documentation
docs.text:
Start with:

  1. Scope of this hand-over
  2. First 30 days
  3. Where everything lives

Then follow the data flow:

  1. Data lifecycle overview — understand the pipeline
  2. Use the Systems section for detailed tool documentation
  3. Use Operations & Governance for recurring tasks and procedures

docs.title: Important note
docs.text:
This hand-over is intentionally written in a practical format. Each task should answer five questions:

  1. Why does this task exist?
  2. When should it be done?
  3. Where is it done?
  4. What are the steps?
  5. What should be checked before the task is considered complete?

docs.title: First 30 days
docs.text: This page is a practical onboarding sequence for the next person taking over the role.

docs.title: Week 1
docs.text:
• Review the data lifecycle overview to understand the pipeline.
  • Obtain access to all core systems and shared folders.
  • Review the regular task rhythm.
  • Confirm where the current source of truth is for participant identifiers.
  • Review the latest task board and any open blockers.

docs.title: Week 2
docs.text:
• Run through one full cycle of the weekly tasks.
  • Validate the current Castor export and reminder workflow.
  • Review at least one device QC example for each major device workflow.
  • Check script locations, dependencies, and versioning status.

docs.title: Week 3
docs.text:
• Review Snowflake or SQL-related setup and current access limitations.
  • Walk through how a data request would be processed end to end.
  • Review the biobank data flow and multicentre transfer dependencies.

docs.title: Week 4
docs.text:
• Update this documentation where reality differs from the written process.
  • Create a short list of immediate risks and quick wins.
  • Confirm who approves changes in operational workflows, code, and governance documents.

docs.title: Minimum access checklist
docs.text:
The next person should confirm access to:

  • shared mailbox
  • participant tracking files
  • Castor EDC
  • device output locations
  • script repository or folders
  • Snowflake or SQL environment
  • documentation folders and SOP locations

docs.title: Role summary
docs.text:
This role supports the NMCB study as a bridge between:

  • study coordination
  • data collection operations
  • data management
  • lightweight data engineering

The work is not only about keeping data tidy. It also includes designing workflows that reduce manual work, improve traceability, and make future data sharing easier.

docs.title: In scope
docs.text:
The current hand-over covers work reflected in the task board, including:

  • recurring operational tasks
  • Castor logic, exports, and reminders
  • device-related data handling and QC
  • scripts for transformation and eligibility
  • data structure design and Snowflake-related work
  • biobank data flow and request procedures
  • SOP and documentation development

docs.title: Out of scope
docs.text:
This hand-over does not attempt to fully document:

  • all scientific decision-making behind variable definitions
  • all institutional approval routes outside the project team
  • deep infrastructure administration that sits fully with central IT

Those dependencies should still be captured in the relevant SOPs, project folders, and contact lists.

docs.title: Handover principle
docs.text:
The next person should be able to do the work without relying on memory-based explanations. For each process, the target is to document:

  • trigger
  • owner
  • systems used
  • inputs
  • step-by-step actions
  • output
  • checks
  • common failure points

docs.title: Where everything lives
docs.text: This page should be completed and kept current. It is often the most useful part of a hand-over.

docs.title: Recommended structure
docs.text:
Use this page as a map of systems, folders, owners, and permissions.

Resource Purpose Current location Owner Notes Shared mailbox Operational follow-up TBD TBD Add mailbox name and delegate setup Visit log Visit tracking TBD TBD Define source of truth Subject ID log Participant ID mapping TBD TBD Critical for joins across systems Castor project Data capture TBD TBD Add project URL and role names Linked2Trial input/output Recruitment screener exchange TBD TBD Note timing expectation Device raw data Raw exports TBD TBD Organise by device and date Device QC outputs QC reports and cleaned outputs TBD TBD Prefer versioned folders Scripts Python/R code TBD TBD Prefer Git version control Snowflake / SQL setup Structured data TBD TBD Record how access is provisioned SOPs Governance and procedures TBD TBD Keep links stable Data request forms External access workflow TBD TBD Add published version

docs.title: Folder design principle
docs.text:
A good storage structure should separate:

  • raw data
  • processed data
  • scripts
  • logs
  • documentation
  • outgoing deliverables

docs.title: Minimum expectation
docs.text: Every critical workflow documented on this site should point back to an actual folder, system, or repository here.

docs.title: Risks and improvement backlog
docs.text: This page turns scattered board items into a manageable improvement backlog.

docs.title: Current recurring risks
docs.text: Risk Why it matters Suggested mitigation Tacit knowledge in operational tasks Handover failure if one person leaves Write task-based instructions and file maps Inconsistent identifiers across systems Broken merges and wrong linkage Maintain subject ID source of truth and checks Device-specific data quirks QC errors and mismatched outputs Standardise conversion docs and mapping tables Hidden logic in Castor calculations Hard-to-debug discrepancies Mirror critical logic in documented scripts Fragile data extraction flow Manual work and inconsistent exports Design patient-centric + source-centric extraction model Limited documentation of SQL/Snowflake setup Slow onboarding and troubleshooting Document access, connection tests, and key tables

docs.title: High priority
docs.text:
• complete the “where everything lives” page
  • create a script inventory
  • define source of truth for identifiers
  • formalise device mapping tables and QC output expectations

docs.title: Medium priority
docs.text:
• add a current-state data pipeline diagram
  • standardise request log and delivery logging
  • create sample examples for common extracts

docs.title: Strategic priority
docs.text:
• move recurring manual transformations into versioned pipelines
  • evaluate a formal analytical data model
  • document OMOP considerations only after core operational flows are stable

docs.title: Data requests
docs.text: The board includes tasks on standardising the data request procedure and developing and publishing sample and data request forms.

docs.title: Goal
docs.text: Create a request process that is clear for researchers and manageable for the team.

docs.title: Minimum request workflow
docs.text:
1. Receive request form.
  2. Check completeness.
  3. Confirm scientific and governance fit.
  4. Confirm requested data or sample scope.
  5. Prepare extract or response.
  6. Record what was shared and when.

docs.title: Minimum form contents
docs.text:
A request form should capture:

  • applicant and institution
  • project title and purpose
  • requested data categories or variables
  • requested sample types if applicable
  • ethical approval status where relevant
  • data transfer and access arrangements
  • expected outputs or publication plan

docs.title: Internal review points
docs.text:
Before release, confirm:

  • the requested data exists
  • the request is in scope
  • identifiers and anonymisation level are appropriate
  • the deliverable can be reproduced if asked again later

docs.title: Suggested deliverables to maintain
docs.text:
• public-facing request form
  • internal review checklist
  • request log
  • standard email templates for approval, revision, and rejection

docs.title: SOPs and documentation
docs.text: The board includes SOP-related work on overview, withdrawal, LDOT operations, and multicentre data transfer.

docs.title: Principle
docs.text:
Operational pages in this site are not a substitute for formal SOPs. They should complement each other.

Use this documentation site for:

  • practical instructions
  • process maps
  • location of files and scripts
  • high-level logic

Use formal SOPs for:

  • approved procedural standards
  • compliance-sensitive actions
  • official version-controlled instructions that require sign-off

docs.title: Recommended SOP index
docs.text: SOP Status Owner Location Withdrawal TBD TBD TBD Multi-centre data transfer TBD TBD TBD LDOT operations TBD TBD TBD

docs.title: Maintenance rule
docs.text: Whenever an SOP changes, review the linked practical documentation page as well. The two should not drift apart.

docs.title: Data lifecycle overview
docs.text: NMCB data flows through five main stages. Understanding this flow helps you know where data comes from, how it is transformed, and where it goes.

docs.title: The pipeline
docs.text:
Collection  →  Storage  →  Cleaning  →  Modelling  →  Distribution
Stage What happens Where Collection Data is captured at source Castor, devices, Linked2Trial Storage Raw and processed data are stored File structure, Snowflake, biobank Cleaning Quality control and transformation Device QC, cleaning scripts Modelling Data is structured for analysis Snowflake, eligibility logic Distribution Data is shared with requesters Data requests, multicentre transfers

docs.title: Core principle
docs.text:
Identifiers and provenance must survive every transformation step.

Every workflow should make it possible to answer:

  • Where did this record originate?
  • Which script or process changed it?
  • Which identifier links it back to the participant or visit?
  • Is the current dataset raw, intermediate, or analysis-ready?

docs.title: Current infrastructure status
docs.text:
The task board shows active work on:

  • building a diagram of the data pipeline
  • designing file structures in the research drive
  • testing SQL setup and Snowflake connectivity
  • standardising conversion code for device-derived data
  • improving extraction flow by combining patient-centric and source-centric approaches
  • exploring automated eligibility logic in Snowflake
  • mapping NMCB data toward OMOP

This suggests the infrastructure is still evolving and should be documented as a living system rather than a finished platform.

docs.title: Next steps
docs.text:
Use the lifecycle pages to understand each stage:

  1. Collection — Castor, devices, Linked2Trial
  2. Storage — File structure, Snowflake, biobank
  3. Cleaning — Device QC, conversion scripts
  4. Modelling — Snowflake structures, eligibility logic
  5. Distribution — Data requests, multicentre transfers

For detailed system documentation, see the Systems reference.

docs.title: Data cleaning
docs.text: This stage covers quality control and transformation of raw data before it is used for analysis.

docs.title: Main cleaning activities
docs.text: Activity Purpose System Device QC Validate and clean device outputs Devices Conversion scripts Standardise formats (Omron, Tanita, Nellcor, M&L) Scripts Python/R cleaning Clean raw data, quality control Scripts

docs.title: Device QC
docs.text:
For each device, document:

  1. setup before use
  2. raw output format
  3. naming convention
  4. quality control procedure
  5. cleaned output or conversion format

docs.title: Device-specific notes
docs.text:
• Nellcor: Date format change from MM/DD/YY to DD/MM/YY — device settings matter for downstream matching.

  • Tanita: Difference between onsite Tanita Pro and portable home-visit Tanita data; document field mappings and how outputs are standardised.

  • VU-AMS: Document expected file pattern, abnormal export patterns, QC script or manual process, and pass/fail criteria.

  • Amsterdam Cognitive Scan: Document QC checks, completeness criteria, and how output links to participant ID.

docs.title: Scripts
docs.text:
Scripts for cleaning and conversion should be documented with:

  • repository or folder location
  • language and package dependencies
  • how to run
  • required inputs
  • produced outputs
  • validation or QC rule

docs.title: Automation opportunities
docs.text:
The board suggests repeated interest in reducing manual work around:

  • raw file cleaning and QC
  • standardising conversion code

These are strong candidates for future formal pipelines rather than person-dependent scripts.

docs.title: Data collection
docs.text: This stage covers how data is captured at source: study forms, device outputs, and recruitment screeners.

docs.title: Main collection sources
docs.text: Source Purpose System Study forms and surveys Participant data, questionnaires Castor EDC Device outputs Physiological measurements Devices (Nellcor, Tanita, VU-AMS, Amsterdam Cognitive Scan) Recruitment screeners Linked2Trial screening flow Linked2Trial exchange

docs.title: Castor EDC
docs.text:
Castor is the main data capture environment for study forms, survey logic, and participant-related study data.

Key hand-over points:

  • Calculations and logic: Document variable dependencies, formula definitions, expected outputs, and known edge cases.

  • Reminders for incomplete screeners: Define the query to identify incomplete cases, export the list, review exclusions, send reminders, and record reminder date and outcome.

  • File uploads: Document allowed file types, naming conventions, size limits, and where uploaded files are retrieved for QC or processing.

docs.title: Devices
docs.text:
Device data is captured during visits. Each device has:

  • setup before use
  • raw output format
  • naming convention
  • quality control procedure (see Cleaning)
  • cleaned output or conversion format

docs.title: Linked2Trial
docs.text: The general screeners should be sent within 48 hours of receiving the participant list.

docs.title: Risks
docs.text:
• Hidden logic in Castor form calculations
  • Inconsistent use of identifiers across systems
  • Reminder processes that are not reproducible
  • Dependence on manual exports without versioning

docs.title: Data distribution
docs.text: This stage covers how data is shared: data requests, multicentre transfers, and extracts.

docs.title: Main distribution channels
docs.text: Channel Purpose Documentation Data requests External access Data requests Multicentre transfers Nijmegen ↔ Amsterdam UMC Biobank Internal extracts Analysis-ready datasets Snowflake, Scripts

docs.title: Data request workflow
docs.text:
1. Receive request form.
  2. Check completeness.
  3. Confirm scientific and governance fit.
  4. Confirm requested data or sample scope.
  5. Prepare extract or response.
  6. Record what was shared and when.

Before release, confirm:

  • the requested data exists
  • the request is in scope
  • identifiers and anonymisation level are appropriate
  • the deliverable can be reproduced if asked again later

docs.title: Multicentre transfers
docs.text:
For data moving between Nijmegen and Amsterdam UMC or other sites, document:

  • transfer trigger
  • transfer frequency
  • allowed transport mechanism
  • file naming and structure
  • expected confirmation back from receiver
  • escalation if files are missing or inconsistent

docs.title: Operational risk
docs.text: Biobank and multicentre workflows are especially vulnerable to confusion when naming conventions, sample identifiers, or template versions are not tightly controlled.

docs.title: Data modelling
docs.text: This stage covers how data is structured for analysis: Snowflake structures, eligibility logic, and OMOP mapping.

docs.title: Main modelling activities
docs.text: Activity Purpose System Snowflake structures Tables, views, analytical queries Snowflake Eligibility logic Inclusion criteria, ME criteria Castor, Scripts OMOP mapping Standardised data model In progress

docs.title: Eligibility scripts
docs.text:
Python/R scripts exist to calculate eligibility in parallel with Castor automatic calculations. This provides an external check.

Document for each script:

  • input export required from Castor
  • exact fields used
  • run command or notebook location
  • expected output structure
  • how discrepancies with Castor are resolved

docs.title: Snowflake
docs.text:
Snowflake is used to potentially move logic such as eligibility into a more controlled environment.

Current board themes:

  • automated eligibility in Snowflake
  • improve extraction flow by combining patient-centric with source-centric views

docs.title: Architecture note
docs.text: Moving logic into Snowflake can be beneficial, but only if business rules remain readable and versioned. Avoid replacing one opaque manual process with an opaque database process.

docs.title: OMOP
docs.text: Mapping NMCB data toward OMOP is on the roadmap. Document OMOP considerations only after core operational flows are stable.

docs.title: Data storage
docs.text: This stage covers where data is stored: file structures, Snowflake, and biobank systems.

docs.title: Storage locations
docs.text: Location Purpose System Research drive File storage, raw and processed data File structure Snowflake Structured data, analytical queries Snowflake Biobank Sample metadata, biosample data Biobank

docs.title: Folder design principle
docs.text:
A good storage structure should separate:

  • raw data
  • processed data
  • scripts
  • logs
  • documentation
  • outgoing deliverables

docs.title: File structure
docs.text:
See Where everything lives for the current map of folders, owners, and permissions.

Key locations include:

  • Device raw data (organised by device and date)
  • Device QC outputs (versioned folders preferred)
  • Scripts (prefer Git version control)
  • Snowflake / SQL setup

docs.title: Snowflake
docs.text:
Snowflake is used for:

  • structuring study data more systematically
  • reducing manual transformations
  • supporting downstream extraction and reporting

Document access model, connection details, and key tables or views.

docs.title: Biobank
docs.text:
Biobank storage includes:

  • sample metadata
  • fecal sample data template
  • biosample data template (except fecal sample)

Template governance is critical: define which template is current, version, owner, and mandatory columns.

docs.title: Operations overview
docs.text: The operational side of the role keeps the study running day to day. It includes recurring coordination tasks and a set of small but critical maintenance activities.

docs.title: Core recurring tasks from the board
docs.text:
• weekly NMCB mailbox review
  • weekly visit log maintenance
  • bi-weekly subject ID log maintenance
  • bi-weekly sending of screeners to Linked2Trial
  • preparation of a routine checklist for recurring data tasks

docs.title: Operational design principle
docs.text:
A recurring task should be documented in a way that makes it easy to execute and easy to audit. Each task page should include:

  • purpose
  • trigger or frequency
  • inputs
  • steps
  • output
  • quality checks
  • escalation route

docs.title: Issue log and escalation
docs.text: A hand-over is incomplete without a simple rule for what to do when a task fails.

docs.title: Escalation model
docs.text: Issue type Example First action Escalate to Access issue Cannot open Castor or shared drive Verify own permissions and recent changes System owner / IT Data integrity issue Duplicate subject IDs Pause downstream merging Data manager Participant workflow issue Screener not sent in time Record impacted participant(s) and fix if possible Study coordinator Device issue Corrupt or missing VU-AMS export Preserve raw files and document issue Device owner / research team Warehouse issue Snowflake connection failure Capture error and test access assumptions Data engineer / IT

docs.title: Minimum issue log fields
docs.text:
When tracking recurring issues, capture at least:

  • date
  • workflow
  • summary of problem
  • impact
  • action taken
  • owner
  • status

docs.title: Principle
docs.text: Do not silently work around structural issues. If a workaround becomes repeated behaviour, it should either be documented as the new process or escalated as a design problem.

docs.title: Regular tasks
docs.text: This page translates the recurring tasks from the board into executable hand-over instructions.

docs.title: Purpose
docs.text: Monitor the shared mailbox and ensure time-sensitive operational items are not missed.

docs.title: Frequency
docs.text: Weekly at minimum. Increase frequency during active recruitment or periods with many participant contacts.

docs.title: Inputs
docs.text:
• access to the shared mailbox
  • current contact list
  • current routing rules or responsibilities

docs.title: Steps
docs.text:
1. Review all unread or newly received messages.
  2. Classify messages into one of the main categories:
  3. participant communication
  4. scheduling or visit follow-up
  5. data issue
  6. device issue
  7. researcher request
  8. administrative request
  9. Forward or assign the message to the responsible person when it is outside your direct ownership.
  10. Record any action needed in the relevant log or tracker.
  11. Flag unresolved items that need follow-up.

docs.title: Output
docs.text:
• mailbox triaged
  • urgent issues routed
  • relevant operational actions logged

docs.title: Quality checks
docs.text:
• no urgent participant-facing email remains unassigned
  • all data-related problems have an owner
  • follow-up deadlines are visible somewhere outside the inbox

docs.title: Purpose
docs.text: Maintain an accurate overview of completed, pending, and problematic visits.

docs.title: Frequency
docs.text: Weekly.

docs.title: Inputs
docs.text:
• current visit planning overview
  • completed visit information from the team
  • visit log source file

docs.title: Steps
docs.text:
1. Compare the planned visits with completed visits.
  2. Update the visit log with visit date, visit type, and status.
  3. Add notes on rescheduling, incomplete procedures, or data issues.
  4. Flag visits where downstream actions are still pending, such as data upload or device return.

docs.title: Output
docs.text: Updated visit log.

docs.title: Quality checks
docs.text:
• no completed visit is missing from the log
  • incomplete visits are visibly flagged
  • downstream data tasks can be derived from the log

docs.title: Purpose
docs.text: Keep participant identifiers consistent across operational and analytical systems.

docs.title: Frequency
docs.text: Bi-weekly, and additionally whenever new participants are added.

docs.title: Inputs
docs.text:
• current enrolment list
  • subject ID log
  • Castor identifiers or other operational IDs

docs.title: Steps
docs.text:
1. Identify all newly included participants.
  2. Assign or verify the subject ID according to the agreed naming convention.
  3. Check for duplicates or accidental reuse.
  4. Confirm the same identifier is used consistently in all dependent systems.
  5. Save the updated log in the agreed source location.

docs.title: Output
docs.text: Updated subject ID master list.

docs.title: Quality checks
docs.text:
• no duplicate IDs
  • no gaps caused by accidental deletion or overwriting
  • mapping remains consistent across logs, Castor, and device outputs

docs.title: Purpose
docs.text: Ensure new participants receive the required general screeners within the expected time window.

docs.title: Context from current board
docs.text: The current note indicates that Laurian provides a participant list regularly and that the general screeners should be sent within 48 hours.

docs.title: Frequency
docs.text: Bi-weekly routine check, but the operational expectation is driven by the 48-hour turnaround after receipt of the list.

docs.title: Inputs
docs.text:
• participant list from Linked2Trial or team contact
  • agreed export or import format
  • access to the receiving system and confirmation method

docs.title: Steps
docs.text:
1. Check whether a new participant list has been received.
  2. Validate that the list has the required identifiers and contact fields.
  3. Prepare the screener input file or upload package.
  4. Send or upload the screener data according to the agreed workflow.
  5. Confirm completion and record the date.
  6. Flag participants who could not be processed.

docs.title: Output
docs.text: Participants transferred into the screener workflow.

docs.title: Quality checks
docs.text:
• 48-hour target met where possible
  • no participant appears twice
  • any failed transfer is logged and visible

docs.title: Purpose
docs.text: Reduce dependence on memory and standardise recurring work.

docs.title: Frequency
docs.text: Update monthly or whenever operational steps change.

docs.title: Steps
docs.text:
1. List recurring tasks by week, month, and ad hoc trigger.
  2. Add owner, backup owner, and expected completion time.
  3. Link each checklist item to the relevant documentation page.
  4. Version the checklist so changes are traceable.

docs.title: Output
docs.text: Operational checklist used for routine work and onboarding.

docs.title: Task template
docs.text: Use this template for every operational or technical task that needs to be handed over.

docs.title: Template
docs.text:
## Task name

### Purpose
Why this task exists.

### Frequency or trigger
When the task should be done.

### Systems and locations
Where the task is performed and where inputs/outputs live.

### Inputs
What is needed before starting.

### Steps
1. Step one
2. Step two
3. Step three

### Output
What should exist when the task is complete.

### Quality checks
How to verify the task was done correctly.

### Common issues
Typical errors or bottlenecks.

### Escalation
Who to contact when the task cannot be completed.

docs.title: Writing guidance
docs.text:
For hand-over quality, write task descriptions so that they are:

  • action-based rather than narrative
  • explicit about inputs and outputs
  • linked to real systems and folders
  • clear about owner and escalation path

docs.title: Best practice
docs.text:
Avoid descriptions like:

Check data and fix issues.

Prefer descriptions like:

Compare the latest Castor export with the previous export, review missing values in required fields, flag records with invalid visit status, and save the issue list in the QC folder.

docs.title: Biobank and multicentre data
docs.text:
Lifecycle stages: Storage (sample metadata); Distribution (multicentre transfers)

The board shows a meaningful cluster of work around biobank flow and multicentre collaboration.

docs.title: Biobank data
docs.text:
• biobank data flow
  • fecal sample data template
  • biosample data template except fecal sample
  • prepare input file for biobank
  • clean dirty ML data
  • script and protocol for biobank data flow
  • two incomplete box

docs.title: Multicentre
docs.text:
• manual of procedures for NMCB
  • sample processing data in Nijmegen
  • lab data and alert in Nijmegen
  • data transfer towards A-UMC

docs.title: 1. Data flow clarity
docs.text:
Document the full biobank flow from source to destination:

  1. where sample metadata originates
  2. how it is cleaned or transformed
  3. what template is used
  4. who receives the prepared file
  5. how completion is confirmed

docs.title: 2. Template governance
docs.text:
If multiple templates exist, define:

  • which template is current
  • version number or date
  • owner of the template
  • mandatory columns
  • validation rules before sending

docs.title: 3. Multicentre transfers
docs.text:
For data moving between Nijmegen and Amsterdam UMC or other sites, document:

  • transfer trigger
  • transfer frequency
  • allowed transport mechanism
  • file naming and structure
  • expected confirmation back from receiver
  • escalation if files are missing or inconsistent

docs.title: Operational risk
docs.text: Biobank and multicentre workflows are especially vulnerable to confusion when naming conventions, sample identifiers, or template versions are not tightly controlled.

docs.title: Castor EDC
docs.text:
Castor is the main data capture environment for study forms, survey logic, and participant-related study data.

Lifecycle stage: Collection (data capture); Modelling (eligibility logic)

docs.title: Current Castor-related task themes from the board
docs.text:
• review of calculations
  • parallel eligibility calculation in Python/R versus Castor JavaScript logic
  • screener reminders for participants with incomplete surveys
  • dependencies and calculation logic for specific questionnaires
  • connection between Castor and Snowflake-oriented workflows
  • evaluation of a new Castor setup for uploading files

docs.title: 1. Calculations and logic
docs.text:
Castor calculations should not be treated as self-evident. Where important calculations exist, document:

  • variable dependencies
  • formula or logic definition
  • expected output values
  • known edge cases
  • whether the same logic exists in an external validation script

docs.title: 2. Eligibility scripts
docs.text:
The board indicates Python/R scripts exist to calculate eligibility in parallel with Castor automatic calculations. This is good practice because it provides an external check.

Document for each script:

  • input export required from Castor
  • exact fields used
  • run command or notebook location
  • expected output structure
  • how discrepancies with Castor are resolved

docs.title: 3. Reminders for incomplete screeners
docs.text:
The board includes a task for reminding 89 participants who had not completed screening surveys. That should become a standard operational workflow rather than a one-off memory-based task.

Suggested workflow elements:

  1. Define the query or filter used to identify incomplete cases.
  2. Export or list the relevant participants.
  3. Review exclusions before sending reminders.
  4. Send reminders through the agreed channel.
  5. Record reminder date and outcome.

docs.title: 4. File uploads in Castor
docs.text:
If Castor is being used for uploading files, document very clearly:

  • allowed file types
  • naming conventions
  • maximum size or system limitations
  • where uploaded files are later retrieved for QC or processing

docs.title: Risks
docs.text:
Main risks in Castor workflows include:

  • hidden logic in form calculations
  • inconsistent use of identifiers
  • reminder processes that are not reproducible
  • dependence on manual exports without versioning

docs.title: Data architecture
docs.text: This page provides a systems-level overview. For a stage-by-stage view of the data flow, see the Data lifecycle.

docs.title: Overview
docs.text:
NMCB data currently spans several operational and analytical domains:

  • study capture and study logic in Castor
  • participant and visit tracking in operational logs
  • recruitment-related handoffs such as Linked2Trial
  • device outputs requiring cleaning or quality control
  • structured data and downstream transformation work in SQL or Snowflake-oriented workflows
  • biobank and multicentre data transfer processes

docs.title: Conceptual flow
docs.text:
Participant contact / visit
    -> operational tracking
    -> Castor data capture
    -> device outputs
    -> cleaning / QC / transformation
    -> structured storage and extract preparation
    -> internal review or external data request delivery

docs.title: Architecture principle
docs.text:
The most important technical rule is that identifiers and provenance should survive every transformation step.

That means every workflow should make it possible to answer:

  • where did this record originate?
  • which script or process changed it?
  • which identifier links it back to the participant or visit?
  • is the current dataset raw, intermediate, or analysis-ready?

docs.title: Current infrastructure topics reflected in the board
docs.text:
The task board shows active work on:

  • building a diagram of the data pipeline
  • designing file structures in the research drive
  • testing SQL setup and Snowflake connectivity
  • standardising conversion code for device-derived data
  • improving extraction flow by combining patient-centric and source-centric approaches
  • exploring automated eligibility logic in Snowflake
  • mapping NMCB data toward OMOP

This suggests the infrastructure is still evolving and should be documented as a living system rather than a finished platform.

docs.title: Devices and QC
docs.text:
Lifecycle stages: Collection (data capture); Cleaning (QC and conversion)

The task board shows several device-related workflows. These need explicit documentation because device data often contains the highest amount of tacit operational knowledge.

docs.title: Device topics visible in the board
docs.text:
• portable Tanita data and mapping differences
  • VU-AMS abnormal report
  • VU-AMS quality control protocol
  • changing Nellcor date format from MM/DD/YY to DD/MM/YY
  • Amsterdam Cognitive Scan QC
  • iPad setup for home visits
  • requisites for using Nellcor
  • Tanita for home visit

docs.title: General rule for device workflows
docs.text:
For each device, document five things:

  1. setup before use
  2. raw output format
  3. naming convention
  4. quality control procedure
  5. cleaned output or conversion format

docs.title: Current board signal
docs.text: There is a setup task and a task specifically about changing the device date format from MM/DD/YY to DD/MM/YY.

docs.title: Handover note
docs.text:
Device settings matter because date formatting mistakes can break downstream matching to visits. Record:

  • exact setup steps
  • screenshot or instruction source
  • verification step before field use

docs.title: Current board signal
docs.text: The board notes a difference between onsite Tanita Pro and portable home-visit Tanita data, with missing mappings.

docs.title: Handover note
docs.text:
This is both a QC and data model issue. The next person should know:

  • what fields differ between device versions
  • whether a field mapping table exists
  • how outputs are standardised before analysis use

docs.title: Current board signal
docs.text: The board notes abnormal reports and a QC protocol. It also mentions that multiple or unexpectedly large files can be created rather than a single expected file.

docs.title: Handover note
docs.text:
Document:

  • expected file pattern
  • known abnormal export patterns
  • whether all files should be preserved
  • which script or manual process is used for QC
  • what constitutes a pass/fail QC result

docs.title: Amsterdam Cognitive Scan
docs.text: Document the QC checks performed, what is considered complete, and how output is linked back to the participant ID.

docs.title: iPads for home visits
docs.text:
If iPads are used for data collection or participant-facing workflows, document:

  • setup checklist
  • installed apps
  • account or login dependencies
  • reset or handover procedure after use

docs.title: Scripts and automation
docs.text:
Lifecycle stages: Cleaning (conversion, QC); Modelling (eligibility); Distribution (extracts)

The board indicates a substantial amount of scripting work in Python and R.

docs.title: Script themes visible in the board
docs.text:
• Python/R scripts for eligibility
  • scripts for ME criteria
  • scripts for inclusion criteria
  • scripts for compiling M&L files and subsetting on request
  • conversion documentation and code for Omron, Tanita, Nellcor, and M&L
  • Python scripts for cleaning raw data and quality control
  • getting numbers and summary counts

docs.title: Recommended script inventory
docs.text:
Every script should be listed in a simple inventory table.

Script Purpose Input Output Owner Status TBD TBD TBD TBD TBD active / draft / deprecated

docs.title: Minimum documentation per script
docs.text:
For each maintained script, capture:

  • repository or folder location
  • language and package dependencies
  • how to run it
  • required inputs
  • produced outputs
  • expected runtime or scale
  • validation or QC rule

docs.title: Good practice for hand-over
docs.text:
Scripts should not be documented as only code references. The next person should be able to answer:

  • when should this script be run?
  • who relies on its output?
  • what happens if it fails?
  • does it overwrite data or create a new version?

docs.title: Automation opportunities reflected in the board
docs.text:
The board suggests repeated interest in reducing manual work, especially around:

  • eligibility logic
  • raw file cleaning and QC
  • extraction flow design
  • standardising conversion code

These are strong candidates for future formal pipelines rather than person-dependent scripts.

docs.title: Snowflake and structured data
docs.text:
Lifecycle stages: Storage; Modelling; Distribution (extracts)

The board includes several tasks related to SQL and Snowflake, including setup testing, connection fixes, and automated eligibility ideas.

docs.title: What Snowflake appears to be used for
docs.text:
Based on the task board, Snowflake is part of the effort to:

  • structure study data more systematically
  • reduce manual transformations
  • support downstream extraction and reporting
  • potentially move logic such as eligibility into a more controlled environment

docs.title: Current board themes
docs.text:
• test SQL setup
  • fix connection with Snowflake
  • SQL knowledge building
  • automated eligibility in Snowflake
  • improve extraction flow by combining patient-centric with source-centric views

docs.title: Hand-over expectations
docs.text: Document the following as soon as possible:

docs.title: Access model
docs.text:
• who grants access
  • which roles exist
  • which environment is used for development versus production-like work

docs.title: Connection details
docs.text:
Do not store secrets here, but document:

  • how credentials are provisioned
  • required driver or package
  • whether VPN or network rules apply
  • a simple connection test procedure

docs.title: Data model
docs.text:
Document key tables or views relevant for NMCB workflows, especially where they are used for:

  • participant-level extracts
  • eligibility logic
  • data request preparation
  • QC summaries

docs.title: Failure handling
docs.text:
When connection problems occur, record:

  • exact error message
  • date/time
  • whether issue was local, credential-based, or server-side
  • whether a fallback workflow was used

docs.title: Architecture note
docs.text: Moving logic into Snowflake can be beneficial, but only if business rules remain readable and versioned. Avoid replacing one opaque manual process with an opaque database process.
